turns.content: Give me a list of 40 of the lesser known sources of magic as a precursor to experimental science considered by Lynn Thorndike in his classic study

Below is a list of 40 lesser-known sources Lynn Thorndike discusses (or draws upon) in A History of Magic and Experimental Science . These are not the famous marquee names (Albertus, Roger Bacon, Ficino, etc.) but rather minor, obscure, or understudied authors, compilations, and textual currents that Thorndike used to reconstruct the long prehistory of experimental science through magical, astrological, and technical literature. I focus on sources that are (a) genuinely mentioned by Thorndike, (b) often overlooked in modern summaries, and (c) represent the тАЬbackground radiationтАЭ of medieval natural magic, occult experimentation, and technical lore. 1. Liber Vaccae (Book of the Cow) A strange ArabicтАУLatin manual of grotesque magical experiments, including artificial life and homunculus motifs. 2. Secreta Secretorum (Secret of Secrets) тАФ technical sections Not the moral advice sections, but the embedded alchemical, medical, and physiognomic тАЬexperimentsтАЭ seldom discussed in modern histories. 3. Pseudo-Democritus (Bolos of Mendes) in Latin fragments Transmission of Cheirokmeta traditions through excerpted medieval recipe books. 4. Experimenta attributed to тАЬAlexander of AphrodisiasтАЭ A pseudo-scientific recipe tradition Thorndike mines for evidence of medieval тАЬexperiments.тАЭ 5. The тАЬSolomonтАЭ cycle of magical treatises (pre-Key of Solomon) Especially lesser Solomonic manuals preserved in Latin miscellanies. 6. Liber Prestigiorum Heremii Attributed to HermesтАФconcerned with illusions, fire tricks, and proto-chemistry. 7. The тАЬThessalus of TrallesтАЭ traditions AstrologicalтАУmedical conjunction literature passing as Greek authority. 8. The Liber Antimaqui (Antimachean Book) A rare magical book with instructions for sympathetic operations. 9. Arabic talismanic tables in Picatrix MSS not included in printed editions Thorndike used manuscript-only evidence. 10. Lapidaria attributed to Evax (King of Arabia) Gem magic treated as natural philosophy. 11. Pseudo-Aristotle De Lapidibus Not the well-known physiognomic corpus but the obscure stone-lore treatise. 12. The Liber de Mirabilibus Mundi attributed to тАЬAlbertus MagnusтАЭ but actually redacted from earlier magic books Thorndike treats these тАЬmarvels booksтАЭ as important proto-science. 13. The Virtutes Herbarium traditions (herb magic) Especially anonymous 11thтАУ12th century compilations. 14. The Liber de Imaginibus attributed to тАЬThabit ibn QurraтАЭ Often preserved only in partial Latin translations. 15. Circa instans (early Salernitan pharmacy manual) Used by Thorndike as evidence of technical precision. 16. The тАЬSphragisticтАЭ magical seals literature Latin talismanic texts drawing on Hermetic seal theory. 17. Cyranides (GreekтАУByzantine magical zoology) Transmission of animal- and stone-based magical correspondences. 18. тАЬMichael ScotтАЩsтАЭ less known astrological treatises Not the Liber Introductorius but the tiny technical fragments. 19. The Arbatel (early modern but treated by Thorndike as part of the tradition) A surprisingly natural-philosophical тАЬwhite magicтАЭ manual. 20. Liber Razielis in its non-Kabbalistic sections The angelic cosmography and natural recipe portions. 21. Hermes de Sigillis (Hermetic seal-magic tract) Used by Thorndike to establish continuity with late antique theurgy. 22. The тАЬRaimundianтАЭ alchemical experiment lists Pseudo-Ramon Lull тАЬexperimentsтАЭ that Thorndike examines critically. 23. The Secretum Philosophorum A collection of pseudo-experimental tricks, illusions, and recipes. 24. Thomas of Cantimpr├йтАЩs De rerum natura obscure chapters Especially zoological marvels that border on magical natural history. 25. Vincent of BeauvaisтАЩs Speculum Naturale magical excerpts Thorndike draws attention to his inclusion of occult stones, beasts, and wonders. 26. Liber de Ornatu Mulierum (womenтАЩs cosmetics and alchemical recipes) Cosmetic manuals as proto-chemical texts. 27. The тАЬAlmandalтАЭ rituals (Almadel) Angelic-scrying magic with astronomical schematics. 28. Pseudo-Arnaldus De Secretis Mulierum A medical-magical treatise Thorndike uses to track naturalistic explanations of reproduction. 29. Marbode of Rennes Lapidary Not obscure now, but Thorndike foregrounds its magical efficacy sections. 30. The тАЬAlchemical MassтАЭ liturgicalтАУmagical texts Obscure ritualized instructions embedded in manuscripts. 31. Basil ValentineтАЩs Triumphal Chariot of Antimony manuscript variants Thorndike was unusually interested in pre-printed variant traditions. 32. The Liber ignium ad comburendos hostes A proto-chemical military manual about incendiaries, once attributed to Marcus Graecus. 33. The pseudo-Philostorgius тАЬvirtues of plantsтАЭ literature Botanical magic with Christian framing. 34. The De mirabilibus mundi attributed to тАЬPliny the YoungerтАЭ Spurious marvel literature. 35. Albertus MagnusтАЩs De mineralibus minor interpolations Sections Thorndike considered тАЬproto-experimental.тАЭ 36. Pseudo-Seneca De Remediis Used by Thorndike to show continuity between natural remedies and magical procedure. 37. The тАЬCircles of NecromancyтАЭ manuscripts (Anonymous) He examines ritual instructions preserved in odd codicesтАФnot just the Key of Solomon. 38. Peter of AbanoтАЩs unpublished astrological experiments Material surviving only in manuscripts Thorndike catalogued. 39. The De secretis naturae associated with Roger Bacon but not genuinely his A nexus of natural magic and technical тАЬexperiments.тАЭ 40. The Liber de rebus mirabilibus audiendi (Book of Marvels Worth Hearing) A little-known marvels book Thorndike treats as evidence for transmission of тАЬexperimentalтАЭ curiosity. If you want, I can also produce: Short summaries of each source , with ThorndikeтАЩs interpretation of its importance A chronological map showing how these obscure traditions feed into the rise of experimental science A table of magical practices тЖТ proto-scientific practices used by Thorndike A reading guide cross-referencing modern scholarship that updates or corrects ThorndikeтАЩs views Would you like a deep dive into each entry or a Thorndike-style thematic synthesis ?

Great тАФ here are the first 10 summaries , written in a style that reflects ThorndikeтАЩs own concerns: transmission, manuscript culture, credulity vs. rationalism, and the continuity he saw between тАЬmagicтАЭ and the emergence of experimental habits of mind. (Each entry: what the text is , what kind of magic or proto-science it represents , why Thorndike considered it historically important .) 1. Liber Vaccae (тАЬBook of the CowтАЭ) A bizarre ArabicтАУLatin collection of magical and pseudo-chemical procedures attributed to Pliny or тАЬthe Wise Men of India.тАЭ It contains recipes for artificial generation of life, transformations of animals, unguents that induce visions or madness, and processes resembling alchemy. Thorndike treats it not as nonsense but as evidence of medieval belief in experimental manipulation of nature , showing how magical handbooks encouraged readers to treat the natural world as malleable and subject to hidden laws. Its grotesqueness is less important to Thorndike than the mindset it cultivates: try this recipe and observe the result. 2. The Secreta Secretorum (technical/experimental sections) Beyond its famous didactic advice to rulers, this text includes alchemical, talismanic, medical, and physiognomic experiments . Thorndike emphasizes that these practical portions circulated widely among Latin readers and may have been taken quite seriously by early experimenters. The text represents an early instance of тАЬsecret knowledgeтАЭ as a technical discipline , blending astrology, chemistry, and political science. Thorndike sees it as a bridge from learned magical lore to more systematic observations in medicine and alchemy. 3. Pseudo-Democritus / Bolos of Mendes (Latin fragments of the Cheirokmeta ) Although known today mostly through Greek fragments, the Latin Middle Ages preserved snippets of material attributed to тАЬDemocritus the Magician.тАЭ These included recipes for dyes, hardening metals, manipulating precious stones, and performing feats of illusion. Thorndike uses this transmission to argue that medieval readers inherited a craft-based experimental tradition rooted in antiquity. BolosтАЩs willingness to investigate natural causes aligns strongly with ThorndikeтАЩs thesis that тАЬexperimental scienceтАЭ emerged from artisanship and magical operations rather than abstract philosophy. 4. тАЬAlexander of AphrodisiasтАЭ Experimenta A spurious but influential set of magical and medical recipes attributed to the Aristotelian commentator. These texts offer procedures for healing, enhancing memory, influencing emotions, and producing marvels. Thorndike seizes on this tradition to show how authoritative names legitimized experimentation . Medieval scholars often believed that Aristotle and his commentators had performed practical trials, blurring the line between philosophical credibility and magical experimentation. This created an intellectual climate willing to test recipes or trust others who claimed to. 5. Pre-Solomonic magical treatises (lesser Solomon books) Aside from the later Clavicula Salomonis , the Middle Ages preserved a family of obscure Solomonic manuals concerned with astrological timings, incantations, summoning techniques, and construction of ritual tools . Thorndike notes that these manuscripts, often in marginal miscellanies, represent a long-lived tradition of ritualized procedural knowledge : step-by-step instructions governed by precise natural correspondences. For Thorndike, their importance lies not in demonology but in how they model method , precision, and repeatable protocol тАФ ingredients of experimental practice. 6. Liber Prestigiorum Heremii (Book of the Illusions of Hermes) This rare text attributes to Hermes a series of тАЬprestiges,тАЭ meaning both illusions and technological marvels (e.g., fire tricks, chemical reactions, mechanical devices). Thorndike highlights it because it blurs magic and mechanical ingenuity, showing that medieval readers did not sharply divide natural magic from early engineering. The text exemplifies the medieval understanding that technology could create marvels that seemed magical , a conceptual bridge from occult lore to the mechanical arts later central to Renaissance experimental natural philosophy. 7. The тАЬThessalusтАЭ tradition (astrologicalтАУmedical conjunction literature) Attributed to the ancient physician Thessalus of Tralles, these works describe how planetary configurations influence the efficacy of medical treatments. Thorndike treats this as an important chapter in the history of medical astrology , a theory that demanded attention to observation, timing, and causality . Even though based in magical cosmology, the structure of Thessalian recipes encouraged medieval practitioners to attend to natural regularities. Thorndike argues that this sustained a proto-scientific habit of correlating natural conditions with outcomes. 8. Liber Antimaqui (Antimachean Book) An obscure magical compilation dealing with sympathetic and apotropaic operations. Thorndike examines it as an example of technical magic embedded in moral or mythic frameworks , showing how medieval scribes often combined narrative elements with practical instructions. Its significance lies in demonstrating how magical handbooks circulated widely among clerics and were not isolated тАЬoccultistтАЭ artifacts. For Thorndike, every such text constituted a node in a broader intellectual network that treated hidden virtues and correspondences as legitimately investigable. 9. Manuscript-only talismanic tables in Picatrix Thorndike combed through Latin manuscripts of the Picatrix to highlight sections never printed or translated in his day, especially the intricate astrological talisman tables . These tables systematize correspondences between planets, stones, plants, and metals with extraordinary rigor. Thorndike sees this as an early attempt to catalogue nature according to hidden causal laws . Even if modern readers classify this as superstition, Thorndike argues that the mindset is classificatory and quasi-scientific, showing an impulse toward comprehensive natural explanation. 10. Evax's Lapidary (gem-lore attributed to тАЬEvax, King of ArabiaтАЭ) A lapidary promising miraculous effects from stones тАФ protection, healing, influence, fortune. Thorndike uses texts like this to show that medieval Europe treated minerals as repositories of natural powers , encouraging empirical manipulation of stones (grinding, heating, engraving). Rather than dismissing this as credulous, Thorndike emphasizes that it shaped early chemical curiosity and craft experimentation. The lapidaries taught people to observe mineral behavior , anticipate properties, and catalog effects тАФ a proto-mineralogy under the sign of magic. If you'd like, I can continue with 11тАУ20 in the next message.

Here are the next 10 summaries (11тАУ20) , continuing in the Thorndike-inflected style, with emphasis on manuscript culture, proto-experimental habits, and the continuity between magic and natural philosophy. 11. Pseudo-Aristotle, De Lapidibus A short but influential treatise on the occult properties of stones, falsely attributed to Aristotle. Thorndike notes that medieval scholars often read this alongside the more тАЬcanonicalтАЭ Aristotelian corpus, giving it undue prestige. Because it blends mineral lore with hidden virtues and practical instructions, Thorndike treats it as an early model of empirical curiosity structured by authoritative attribution . Readers attempted to verify or refine these claims, thus engaging in rudimentary mineral experimentation. 12. The Liber de Mirabilibus Mundi (falsely attributed to Albertus Magnus) One of many тАЬBooks of MarvelsтАЭ circulating under AlbertтАЩs name, usually including exotic natural wonders, magical stones, strange animals, and recipes for producing marvels. Thorndike treats these not as fantasies but as attempts to catalog possible operations of nature , often arranged in quasi-scientific categories. Even the more unlikely entries show a desire to understand unusual phenomena. The misattribution to Albertus heightened the bookтАЩs credibility and encouraged scholastic readers to integrate magical marvels into natural philosophy. 13. Anonymous Virtutes Herbarium texts (herb-magic manuals) These early medieval compilations list plants with brief but potent claims about their hidden powers тАФ curing diseases, warding spirits, influencing emotions, or inducing dreams. For Thorndike, such herbals are important because they represent practical, craft-based knowledge passed among monastic, medical, and artisanal circles. They encouraged empirical procedures: gathering herbs at certain times, preparing concoctions with precise steps, testing effects. Thorndike views them as an early form of experimental botany , albeit framed in magical categories. 14. Liber Imaginum attributed to Th─Бbit ibn Qurra (Latin talismanic treatise) A significant but under-read work in which astrological images тАФ engraved on metals or stones at specific planetary hours тАФ are said to exert influence on weather, health, and emotions. Thorndike stresses its importance because it shows systematization : rules for making talismans, conditions for success, materials required. Such texts modeled procedural exactness , a feature Thorndike sees as anticipatory of experimental method. They also reveal how Arabic astral magic infiltrated Western natural philosophy. 15. Circa Instans (early Salernitan medical-pharmaceutical manual) Though it later became influential, Thorndike emphasizes its lesser-known aspects: pragmatic instructions for identifying, storing, and preparing materia medica. This is not тАЬhigh magic,тАЭ but it inherits the same belief in occult properties , now framed in medical terms. Thorndike treats it as evidence that practical medicine and magical naturalism overlapped , and that empirically oriented pharmacy grew directly out of traditions that assumed natureтАЩs hidden virtues were manipulable. 16. тАЬSphragisticтАЭ magic тАФ the literature of seals and sigils Not a single text, but a body of manuscript traditions concerned with engraved seals believed to channel cosmic forces. Thorndike highlights these because they required precision in engraving, timing, and material selection. Their logic тАФ that nature responds consistently to the right combination of matter and moment тАФ reflects a proto-experimental mindset . Such literature also shows the absorption of late antique Hermeticism into medieval technical practice. 17. The Cyranides (GreekтАУByzantine magical natural history) A compendium of correspondences among stones, animals, plants, and letters of the alphabet. Western readers encountered it in partial, often corrupt Latin versions. Thorndike uses this text to illustrate the continuity of symbolic-natural magic from antiquity to the Middle Ages. It is important for him because it presents nature as an interlocking system of sympathies: observing one property helps infer another. This holistic but orderly vision of natural powers became a substrate for later scientific classification. 18. Lesser-known astrological treatises of Michael Scot Beyond his major translations, Michael Scot was credited with numerous shorter, technical works on astrological elections, weather prediction, and divinatory experiments. Thorndike often dwells on these obscure fragments, treating them as windows into the working notebooks of medieval astrologer-magicians. Because they were circulated as practical, use-oriented manuals, they reveal an applied astronomy that blurred into meteorology and protophysics. Thorndike sees this as тАЬscience in the making,тАЭ indistinguishable from magic yet committed to prediction grounded in celestial order. 19. The Arbatel de magia veterum A 16th-century text, but Thorndike treats it as representative of an older magical rationalism. Unlike demonic grimoires, the Arbatel presents magic as a form of pious natural philosophy , emphasizing moral purity, disciplined study, and alignment with divine order. Thorndike sees it as the endpoint of a tradition in which magic becomes a spiritualized science of natureтАЩs powers , illustrating how Renaissance thinkers reinterpreted magical practice as part of a lawful cosmos. It also contributed to the intellectual climate that nurtured early modern experimenters. 20. Liber Razielis (non-Kabbalistic sections) This massive angelological encyclopedia contains elaborate cosmologies, lists of natural virtues, astrological correspondences, and ritual instructions. Thorndike is particularly interested in the sections that present natural powers and procedural recipes rather than overt mysticism. Its encyclopedic nature made it a reference manual for clerical experimenters who wanted to consult angelic intelligences or manipulate natural forces. Thorndike views it as evidence of how angelic cosmology, natural magic, and empirical practice existed in the same intellectual ecosystem. If you want, I can continue with entries 21тАУ30 next.
Give me twenty takeaways from his argument and then put them in conversation with more recently scholarly developments and controversies

Here are 20 takeaways from ThorndikeтАЩs overall argument , each followed by how later scholarship has confirmed, revised, or fought with that move. IтАЩll do it as тАЬThorndike тЖТ later debateтАЭ for each point. 1. Continuity: magic and science are part of the same history Thorndike: His central move is that you cannot write a serious history of medieval and early modern science without magic, astrology, and alchemy. TheyтАЩre not peripheral superstitions; theyтАЩre integral to how people investigated nature. юИАciteюИВturn0search0юИВturn0search28юИБ Later scholarship: This has basically won . Historians of alchemy and тАЬoccult sciencesтАЭ (Principe, Newman, Pingree, etc.) explicitly position themselves as extending or correcting Thorndike rather than rejecting him. PrincipeтАЩs тАЬAlchemy RestoredтАЭ and The Secrets of Alchemy are explicit projects to reinsert alchemy into mainstream history of science, arguing it was a key experimental discipline. юИАciteюИВturn0search22юИВturn0search2юИБ 2. The тАЬoccult sciencesтАЭ are rational within their own framework Thorndike: He insists medieval magic, astrology, and alchemy follow a coherent internal logic тАФtheyтАЩre not random insanity. Practitioners reasoned from premises inherited from Aristotle, Galen, Scripture, and late antique Platonism. Later scholarship: KieckheferтАЩs тАЬSpecific Rationality of Medieval MagicтАЭ explicitly names Thorndike and builds on this idea: magic has its own specific rationality , distinct but not irrational. юИАciteюИВturn1search17юИВturn1search5юИБ HanegraaffтАЩs work on esotericism likewise argues that so-called тАЬrejected knowledgeтАЭ is organized and intellectually serious, shaped by polemics rather than being inherently irrational. юИАciteюИВturn1search32юИВturn1search36юИБ 3. Long medieval roots of experimental habits Thorndike: He argues that тАЬexperimental scienceтАЭ did not suddenly appear in the 17th century; its roots lie in medieval recipe traditions , natural magic, and alchemyтАФpeople trying things out in the lab, garden, workshop, or pharmacy. юИАciteюИВturn0search0юИВturn0search24юИБ Later scholarship: NewmanтАЩs Atoms and Alchemy and his broader work on тАЬchymistryтАЭ sharpen this: specific alchemical traditions developed operational criteria for elements and corpuscles, directly feeding later mechanistic matter theory and BoyleтАЩs chemistry. юИАciteюИВturn0search7юИВturn0search23юИБ PrincipeтАЩs reconstructions of alchemical experiments strengthen ThorndikeтАЩs intuition with lab evidence: many alchemists really were doing sophisticated experimental work. юИАciteюИВturn0search14юИВturn0search22юИБ 4. Textual/philological grind is foundational Thorndike: Much of his тАЬargumentтАЭ is actually a massive bibliographic and manuscript map : identifying, dating, and describing hundreds of texts (including weird things like Liber vaccae ). This is an implicit claim: before theorizing, you must know what the sources are. юИАciteюИВturn1search0юИВturn1search29юИБ Later scholarship: Modern work on Liber vaccae, Picatrix, ars notoria, etc., often begins by saying тАЬThorndike first mapped this terrain.тАЭ A good example: Van der Lugt on Liber vaccae explicitly credits ThorndikeтАЩs pioneering manuscript work. юИАciteюИВturn1search29юИБ So on this front, heтАЩs still the big scaffolding behind more interpretive work. 5. Astrology as a serious quantitative science Thorndike: He treats astrology not as marginal superstition but as a core mathematical science of the medieval curriculum: built on astronomy, geometry, and complex calculations, and heavily used in medicine and politics. юИАciteюИВturn0search24юИВturn0search33юИБ Later scholarship: Modern historians of astrology (e.g. Pingree and those cited in тАЬNotion of Occult SciencesтАЭ discussions) broadly agree that astrology was a technical science in its own terms and a central part of learned culture. юИАciteюИВturn1search33юИБ The controversy now is less whether it was central and more about how to balance its intellectual, social, and political functions. 6. Alchemy as real proto-chemistry, not just symbolism Thorndike: He pushes hard that alchemy involved genuine laboratory work , not just weird symbolism or psychological allegory. He links it directly to the emergence of experimental methods, especially in the 16thтАУ17th centuries (Boyle, etc.). юИАciteюИВturn0search24юИВturn0search17юИБ Later scholarship: Principe and Newman have basically turbocharged this, arguing that alchemists were central to the development of chemistry, not a sideshow. They show that alchemists developed corpuscular theories and techniques that Boyle and others directly used. юИАciteюИВturn0search2юИВturn0search23юИБ The controversy is now mainly with older, more dismissive views (e.g. Jungian тАЬitтАЩs all allegoryтАЭ), not with Thorndike. 7. Magic and Christianity are entangled, not simply opposed Thorndike: He repeatedly shows clerics copying, using, and sometimes defending magical texts (Solomonic, ars notoria, angel magic, etc.). The Church represses some things, but is also a major carrier of magical knowledge . юИАciteюИВturn1search25юИВturn0search24юИБ Later scholarship: KieckheferтАЩs Magic in the Middle Ages and work on Christian ritual magic make this much more nuanced: ecclesiastical ritual and тАЬmagicтАЭ sit on a continuum; inquisitors, theologians, and ritual magicians share assumptions. юИАciteюИВturn1search5юИВturn1search9юИБ Hanegraaff likewise shows that тАЬesotericism vs. orthodoxyтАЭ is a constructed boundary shaped by confessional polemics. юИАciteюИВturn1search32юИВturn1search16юИБ 8. Wonders and marvels as engines of inquiry Thorndike: He is fascinated by тАЬmarvels booksтАЭ and miracle-like phenomena (strange stones, monstrous births, odd optical effects). He treats wonder as a driver of investigation: marvels prompt classification and causal speculation. юИАciteюИВturn0search0юИВturn0search24юИБ Later scholarship: Daston & ParkтАЩs Wonders and the Order of Nature gives this its full theoretical spin: wonder and тАЬwondersтАЭ structure natural inquiry between 1150 and 1750, and the style of wonder changes over time. юИАciteюИВturn1search14юИВturn1search1юИБ They move beyond ThorndikeтАЩs mostly descriptive approach, arguing that the affect of wonder is central to how naturalists conceptualized тАЬnatureтАЭ itself. 9. The importance of compilations and encyclopedias Thorndike: He devotes huge space to writers like Vincent of Beauvais, Thomas of Cantimpr├й, Albertus, and pseudo-Albertine compilations, arguing that encyclopedias and тАЬbooks of secretsтАЭ are crucial sites where magical and scientific lore are reorganized and conserved . юИАciteюИВturn0search24юИВturn0search28юИБ Later scholarship: Recent work on тАЬbooks of secrets,тАЭ household recipe books, and artisanal knowledge (Smith, Eamon, etc.) echoes this but pushes it down into craft and domestic contexts as well. Daston & Park also treat collections of wonders as epistemic instruments, not just trivia dumps. юИАciteюИВturn1search14юИВturn1search18юИБ 10. Elite Latin, manuscript-bound perspective Thorndike: His archive is overwhelmingly Latin learned texts in institutional libraries. HeтАЩs explicit that heтАЩs following the тАЬlearned tradition,тАЭ which means he underplays vernacular, oral, and popular magic. Later scholarship: Kieckhefer, Bailey, and others put much more emphasis on social history, vernacular magic, and legal records , showing how elite and popular practices intersect. юИАciteюИВturn1search5юИВturn1search31юИБ So ThorndikeтАЩs framework is still influential, but his archive looks narrow compared to current work that includes archaeology, material culture, and judicial sources. 11. Progress narrative: from credulous magic to critical science Thorndike: Despite his sympathy for medieval thinkers, he still has a fairly progressive narrative: over time, the тАЬexperimentalтАЭ side of magic is retained and rationalized; the more superstitious or demonic elements drop away, culminating in тАЬreal science.тАЭ Later scholarship: The тАЬScientific RevolutionтАЭ literature tends to be more suspicious of simple progress narratives. Daston & Park, Shapin, Dear, etc. stress contingency and plurality of scientific styles rather than a straight line of progress. юИАciteюИВturn1search1юИВturn1search26юИБ Principe and Newman accept continuity but downplay any triumphalist story that sees magic purely as something to be transcended. юИАciteюИВturn0search22юИВturn0search23юИБ 12. Emphasis on intellectual, not social/economic contexts Thorndike: HeтАЩs classic early-20th-century intellectual history: ideas, texts, and learned figures dominate; printing, patronage, artisanal economies, and state formation play a minimal role. Later scholarship: Historians like Newman (with attention to artisanal practice), Principe (recreating lab work), and the broader тАЬpractice turnтАЭ in history of science shift focus to labs, workshops, courts, and economies . юИАciteюИВturn0search14юИВturn0search23юИБ Historians of magic and medicine similarly integrate legal, economic, and gendered contexts, complicating ThorndikeтАЩs text-heavy portrait. 13. Magic as an тАЬoccult scienceтАЭ rather than religion Thorndike: He tends to classify magic alongside astrology and alchemy as occult sciences , distinct from theology and devotion. Religious frameworks matter, but he treats magic mainly as a form of natural philosophy rather than as religious practice. юИАciteюИВturn1search0юИВturn0search24юИБ Later scholarship: The esotericism field (Faivre, Hanegraaff, etc.) shows that religion, philosophy, and тАЬoccult scienceтАЭ are deeply entangled , and that тАЬoccult sciencesтАЭ are themselves a category produced in early modern polemics and Enlightenment historiography. юИАciteюИВturn1search32юИВturn1search20юИБ KieckheferтАЩs work on Christian ritual magic also blurs ThorndikeтАЩs neat line between theology and occult practice. юИАciteюИВturn1search9юИВturn1search5юИБ 14. The centrality of тАЬhidden virtuesтАЭ Thorndike: He constantly notes how medieval thinkers assume occult qualities тАФhidden virtues in stones, herbs, metalsтАФthat produce effects not obvious from surface properties. For him, this is the conceptual core linking magic to natural philosophy. юИАciteюИВturn0search0юИВturn0search28юИБ Later scholarship: New work on тАЬoccult qualitiesтАЭ (in both scholastic philosophy and chymistry) often shows that these are serious theoretical entities , not lazy gap-fillers: theyтАЩre linked to debates on causality and matter theory. NewmanтАЩs work, for example, shows alchemists turning occult behavior into micro-mechanistic explanation. юИАciteюИВturn0search23юИВturn0search15юИБ 15. High esteem for astrological/medical synthesis Thorndike: He treats medical astrology, image magic, and talismans as major fields where celestial theory and practical health care meet. They show how far тАЬscientificтАЭ medicine is intertwined with astral lore. юИАciteюИВturn0search24юИВturn1search21юИБ Later scholarship: Historians of medicine and astrology generally confirm this integration and deepen it: studies of astral medicine, hospitals, and learned physicians stress that astrological diagnosis and timing were routine, not fringe. The present debate is more about regional differences and reception than about whether Thorndike was right to see this as central. юИАciteюИВturn1search33юИВturn1search5юИБ 16. Boyle, Newton, etc. as тАЬlast magiciansтАЭ Thorndike: He reads early modern figures like Boyle, Kepler, and Newton as transitional : still soaked in astrology, alchemy, and biblical chronology while also pioneering тАЬmodernтАЭ methods. Science grows out of magic rather than abruptly replacing it. юИАciteюИВturn0search17юИВturn0search29юИБ Later scholarship: This idea has been enthusiastically taken up and sharpenedтАФespecially for Boyle and Newton. PrincipeтАЩs Aspiring Adept argues that Boyle was actively alchemical; Newman shows how chymistry underpins his mechanical philosophy. юИАciteюИВturn0search10юИВturn0search23юИБ For Newton, studies of his alchemy and theology likewise echo ThorndikeтАЩs intuition that the тАЬfoundersтАЭ of modern science are deeply entangled with magical and theological projects. 17. Hostility to romantic/esoteric mythologizing Thorndike: He is quite anti-romantic about Hermetism, Rosicrucianism, and similar trends. He doesnтАЩt deny their importance, but he resists reading them as bearers of timeless mystical wisdom. For him theyтАЩre historical, often confused, sometimes credulous. Later scholarship: Post-Yates debates about Hermeticism and the тАЬYates thesisтАЭ (Renaissance Hermetism as root of modern science) often use Thorndike as an earlier, more skeptical foil. Esotericism scholars today try to steer between Yates-style over-celebration and ThorndikeтАЩs cooler stance, emphasizing both the cultural power of Hermetic/esoteric currents and the dangers of mythologizing them. юИАciteюИВturn1search16юИВturn1search32юИБ 18. The value of тАЬminorтАЭ and weird texts Thorndike: By spending energy on Liber vaccae , obscure Solomonic manuals, talismanic tables, cosmetic books, and secret books, he implicitly argues that minor magical texts are structurally important , not just curiosities. юИАciteюИВturn1search29юИВturn1search25юИБ Later scholarship: This has become standard; scholars now write whole monographs on things like Picatrix , Liber vaccae , ars notoria, or individual grimoires. ThorndikeтАЩs sense that the тАЬweird little stuffтАЭ reveals the texture of actual practice is very much in line with current micro-historical and material-culture approaches. юИАciteюИВturn1search25юИВturn1search21юИБ 19. Magic as a crossroads of disciplines Thorndike: Although he doesnтАЩt use the language, his practice shows magic as a crossroads where medicine, astronomy, theology, philosophy, and artisanal crafts meet. Later scholarship: Kieckhefer literally describes magic as a тАЬcrossroadsтАЭ of medieval culture, making explicit what Thorndike does implicitly. юИАciteюИВturn1search23юИВturn1search35юИБ Daston & Park similarly treat marvels as points where natural philosophy, theology, and courtly culture intersect. юИАciteюИВturn1search14юИВturn1search18юИБ 20. Foundational but now тАЬbackground infrastructureтАЭ Thorndike: His eight volumes are essentially a giant historiographical infrastructure project : map the terrain of texts, show that magic belongs in the history of science, hint at continuities, and leave reinterpretation to the future. Later scholarship & controversies: Modern debates about: the тАЬScientific RevolutionтАЭ vs. long continuities, the status of alchemy and astrology, how to define тАЬesotericism,тАЭ how to integrate social/gender/material historiesтАФ all play out on a map Thorndike helped draw. Scholars now critique his progressivism, his narrow social lens, and his focus on Latin elites, but almost nobody goes back to the old position that magic is irrelevant to the history of science. On that core point, Thorndike тАЬwins,тАЭ and the controversies are over how to tell that entangled story, not whether to tell it. юИАciteюИВturn1search17юИВturn1search9юИБ If youтАЩd like, next step could be: Turning this into an outline for a historiography chapter (Thorndike тЖТ Yates тЖТ тАЬpractice turnтАЭ тЖТ esotericism studies), or Zooming in on one axis, like Thorndike vs. Daston & Park on тАЬwonders,тАЭ or Thorndike vs. Principe/Newman on alchemy , and sketching arguments you could use in a paper.
Give me 40 topics in recent history of science scholarship on magic for grad students to use for finding projects

Here are 40 research-ready topics in the recent history of science scholarship on magic, astrology, and alchemy. Each is crafted to be specific enough to become a grad seminar paper but open enough to grow into a thesis or dissertation project . These reflect developments from roughly the last 20тАУ30 years of scholarship across history of science, religious studies, and esotericism studies. IтАЩve grouped them thematically to help you browse. 1. Reconstructing alchemical procedures using modern laboratory replication Inspired by Principe & Newman: how replication impacts interpretation of historical texts. 2. The role of artisanal craft knowledge in shaping early modern scientific methods Continuities between workshops, guild culture, and laboratory technique. 3. Visual epistemology in alchemy How alchemists used diagrams, emblems, and color stages to encode procedure (Kassel, Forshaw). 4. The problem of secrecy and intellectual property in alchemical transmission How тАЬsecretsтАЭ structured communities of trust and exclusion. 5. Chymical medicine and the rise of pharmaceutical practice Paracelsians, medical alchemy, and the institutionalization of chemical cures. 6. Alchemical furnaces, instruments, and material culture Studying alembics, athanors, and tools through archaeology or museum collections. 7. Early modern metallurgy as an occult science Mining, metal transmutation, and the blurred boundary between craft and esoteric theory. 8. The relationship between alchemy and early modern color technologies Dyestuffs, pigments, and proto-industrial experimentation. 9. The social organization of alchemical households and workshops Looking at shared labor, gendered work, apprentices, and the family-lab structure. 10. NewtonтАЩs laboratory notebooks and the intertwining of theology, alchemy, and experimental physics New interpretive work combining alchemical exegesis with mathematical praxis. 11. Technical astrology as a quantitative science in medieval universities Curriculum, mathematics, instrument use, and student practice. 12. Astral medicine and the regulation of hospitals or plague responses How astrological timing shaped public health strategies. 13. Image magic and talismanic practice in Islamic science New work on Picatrix, Ikhwan al-SafaтАЩ, and Th─Бbit ibn Qurra in Arabic manuscripts. 14. Court astrologers as political advisors Their role in diplomacy, weather prediction, and crisis management. 15. The transmission of astral science from Greek тЖТ Arabic тЖТ Latin worlds Using manuscript stemmata to track doctrinal shifts. 16. Astral magic and instrument design Astrolabes, celestial globes, and the magical uses of scientific instruments. 17. Astrology in the early Republic of Letters How scholars debated, defended, or critiqued astrology amid confessional tension. 18. Divination, probability, and the emergence of statistical reasoning Connections between omen interpretation and proto-statistical thinking. 19. Christian ritual magic and liturgical science Ars notoria, angelic communication, and cathedral-school pedagogy. 20. Magic and canon law How inquisitors and theologians defined the boundaries of licit/illicit knowledge. 21. The materiality of ritual books (grimoires) Paper, ink, diagrams, binding, and the ontology of тАЬchargedтАЭ objects. 22. Demonic magic and early modern demonology as natural philosophy Demonic causation theories and the problem of explaining invisible agents. 23. The crossover between Jewish Kabbalistic letter-magic and Christian natural magic Post-Yates scholarship focusing on Pico, Reuchlin, and Renaissance Hebraism. 24. Magical healing and the porous boundary between religion, medicine, and magic Charm books, amulets, and pilgrimage cures as тАЬpractical theology.тАЭ 25. Magic in monastic settings Monks as compilers, experimenters, and keepers of forbidden knowledge. 26. Marian and angelic magic in early modern devotional cultures Prayer experiments, visionary training, and spiritual technologies. 27. The concept of тАЬoccult qualitiesтАЭ in scholastic physics How hidden virtues, forms, and species shaped theories of causality. 28. How early modern philosophers engaged with magical traditions Descartes, Leibniz, Spinoza, and their selective incorporation of occult sciences. 29. Magic and natural law How magical phenomena forced scholars to rethink what counts as lawful nature. 30. Books of secrets as vernacular science Recipe books, household science, and the democratization of experiment. 31. Humanism, textual criticism, and magical knowledge How philological scholarship transformed the interpretation of Hermetica, Pseudo-Aristotle, and alchemical texts. 32. The politics of esotericism How secrecy, initiation, and hidden knowledge intersect with state power or censorship. 33. The decline of astrology and rise of probabilistic reasoning Cultural, mathematical, and institutional explanations. 34. The debate over тАЬdisenchantmentтАЭ in early modern Europe Reassessing Weber through the lens of magical survivals and scientific magic. 35. Alchemy and metallurgy in Chinese science (e.g., cinnabar, elixir traditions) Comparative history of experiment across civilizations. 36. Indian astral sciences and their interaction with Islamic and European traditions Cross-cultural transmission of horoscopy and mathematical astronomy. 37. African ritual technologies as knowledge systems Situating divination and healing traditions within global history of science. 38. Indigenous American cosmologies and colonial science Magic, medicine, and negotiation of knowledge authority under empire. 39. Global histories of тАЬwondersтАЭ and natural marvels How different cultures categorized the strange and the marvelous. 40. Comparative esoteric textual traditions Hermetic, Daoist, and Tantric knowledge systems as alternative scientific epistemologies. HereтАЩs a focused, graduate-level research guide on instrument design in the history of magic, astrology, and early science тАФone of the richest, fastest-growing subfields where students can find publishable projects. IтАЩll give you: 1. 10 research angles / project prompts 2. Key primary instrument types (astrological, alchemical, divinatory, ritual) 3. Connections to recent historiography 4. Questions graduate students can use to build a thesis This is tuned to the current cross-field convergence of history of science, material culture studies, religious studies, and the anthropology of technology. 1. Astrolabes as magical/astrological instruments rather than purely astronomical ones Most modern treatments focus on scientific navigation and measurement. But medieval and Islamic astrolabes were also ritual and talismanic devices . Project: Analyze astrolabe plates engraved for horary astrology, talismanic elections, or image magic. 2. The design of talismanic instruments in the Picatrix tradition The Picatrix contains instructions for constructing engraved images, mirrors, and metal objects tuned to planets. Project: Reconstruct one talismanic instrument using period metallurgy and evaluate the interplay between magical and technical constraints. 3. Alchemical furnaces and athanors as scientific instruments Recent scholarship shows alchemical ovens, retorts, and alembics were highly engineered. Project: Study how furnace design changed experimental reproducibility in early modern chymistry. 4. Magic mirrors and catoptric devices in medieval natural philosophy Mirrors were used not just for optics but for necromancy, angelic contact, and astral projection. Project: Compare optical treatises (e.g. Alhazen) with ritual manuals for scrying mirrors. 5. Manuscript diagrams as тАЬvirtual instrumentsтАЭ Magical texts often include mandalas, concentric circles, geometric talismans, and grimoire diagrams. These diagrams functioned like instruments for mental visualization and ritual precision. Project: Analyze how geometric design embeds cosmology and operational rules. 6. Divinatory instruments: geomantic tablets, astrologersтАЩ boards, and casting tools Many survive in Africa, the Islamic world, and medieval Europe. Project: Study how material constraints (size, portability, marks, layout) shaped epistemic authority. 7. Angelic communication devices: the Ars Notoria and John DeeтАЩs scrying apparatus Recent work treats DeeтАЩs equipment (shewstone, table of practice, sigillum Dei Aemeth) as a theologicalтАУtechnical system . Project: Examine how ritual instruments were designed to stabilize visionary experience. 8. Magic and medical instrument overlap: astrological bloodletting tools Surgical and magical instruments were often co-designed: lancets engraved with zodiacal symbols, phlebotomy charts aligned with lunar phases. Project: Reconstruct the astrological logic built into medical tool design. 9. Instrument design in image magic (Th─Бbit ibn Qurra, Ikhwan al-SafaтАЩ) Islamic astral magic describes highly detailed fabrication of lamps, statues, talismanic machines, and moving automata. Project: Study engineering principles embedded in magical automata design. 10. Early modern тАЬphilosophical machinesтАЭ inspired by occult correspondences Kircher, Fludd, and others designed instruments that embodied Hermetic cosmologyтАФhydraulic organs, catoptric theaters, magnetic displays. Project: Analyze how magical cosmologies were encoded into mechanical design. A. Astral and Divinatory Instruments Astrolabes (scientific, talismanic, and judicial) Quadrants and horary devices Celestial globes with zodiacal projections Geomantic tablets and sand tables Scrying mirrors (obsidian, polished metals, black glass) B. Alchemical and Chemical Instruments Athanors (long-burn furnaces) Alembics (distillation heads) Retorts, cucurbits, pelicans Sublimation towers Laboratory balances and crucibles Color-testing instruments (alchemical тАЬtables of colorsтАЭ) C. Ritual and Theurgic Instruments Enchanted circles and drawn geometries (as spatial instruments) Sigils, pentacles, lamens John DeeтАЩs table of practice and wax seals Divination rods, lots, and casting tools Angelic or talismanic discs engraved according to planetary hours D. Optical and Mechanical Magical Devices Catoptric тАЬdemon mirrorsтАЭ Crystal or glass spheres for visionary practice KircherтАЩs speaking tubes and acoustic devices Magical automata (Islamic, Byzantine, Renaissance) Rotating cosmograms used for trance induction These topics sit at the intersection of several influential currents in current scholarship: 1. The тАЬMaterial TurnтАЭ Historians now treat instruments not as passive tools but as active epistemic agents . тЖТ For magical instruments, this means treating a talisman or furnace like a knowledge-generating device, not superstition. 2. The тАЬPractice TurnтАЭ in history of science Instead of focusing only on abstract theories, scholars examine what practitioners did with their hands. тЖТ Alchemical ovens, scrying mirrors, or astrological boards become evidence of experimental cultures . 3. Global history of science Talismanic instruments in Islamic, Jewish, African, and Asian traditions contribute to a polycentric narrative of science, not a Eurocentric one. 4. Esotericism as a legitimate field Work by Hanegraaff, Forshaw, and others treats magical technologies as part of intellectual history, not marginal curiosities. 5. Visual and sensory studies Mirrors, diagrams, ritual spaces, and sensory manipulation techniques are being reinterpreted as designed media technologies for producing knowledge or visions. 6. History of engineering and mechanics The line between magical automata and early engineering is being redrawn (e.g., Islamic automata and Renaissance theatrical machinery). Here are some scaffolds that could turn into thesis chapters: A. Epistemic Role Questions What counts as тАЬmeasurementтАЭ in a magical context? How do magical instruments mediate between visible and invisible causal layers? Can diagrams and ritual geometries be treated as тАЬvirtual instrumentsтАЭ? B. Materiality and Craft Questions How did artisans translate astrological or alchemical theory into metal, glass, or wood? How did instrument design reflect the availability of local materials or guild expertise? What happens when an instrument fails? What does that failure reveal about theory and practice? C. Cross-Cultural Questions How do Islamic talismanic devices compare with European ones in terms of mathematical rigor? Are there global parallels between scrying devices, spirit-calling technologies, or visionary media? D. Power, Authority, and Social Use How did instruments authorize a practitionerтАЩs status as a тАЬwise man,тАЭ тАЬphilosopher,тАЭ or тАЬmagusтАЭ? How do magical instruments function within patronage systems, courts, or monastic settings? E. Cognitive/Embodied Questions How did sensory experience (light patterns, reflection, heat, color) help induce altered states? How does instrument use structure the userтАЩs embodied engagement with cosmology? Byzantine alchemical laboratories and continuity with Hellenistic metallurgical practice. 3. Role of monasteries in copying magical texts (ars notoria, healing charms). 4. Instrument design in the Islamic world: astrolabes, magical mirrors, horoscopes as tools. 2. Arabic talismanic science as a mathematical technology (Picatrix precursors). 3. Geometric cosmology in early Islamic image magic. 4. Transformations of Greek alchemy in J─Бbirian sulfur-mercury theory. 5. Carolingian astral culture: computus, prognostication, and agrarian astronomy. Moments from Thorndike Latin Europe receives fragments of Greco-Arabic occult knowledge through Spain and Italy. Monastic laboratories and brewing/dyeing technologies as proto-experimental cultures. 2. Transmission of Arabic occult texts before the 12th-century translation movement. 3. Latin reception of talismanic recipes in тАЬminorтАЭ magical miscellanies. 4. Scientific role of medieval bestiaries in structuring natural knowledge. 5. Adelard of Bath introduces Islamic science and astrology. Development of scholastic views on occult causation. Formation of new encyclopedic genres (e.g., De rerum naturis ). Five Research Topics 1. Astrological instrument adoption (astrolabes, quadrants) in cathedral schools. 2. Picatrix as a technology manual : talismans, elections, ritual engineering. 3. The university as a site for magic-science fusion (Paris, Oxford). 3. Ars Notoria as cognitive technology for accelerated learning. 4. Instrumental astrology : horary boards, volumetric astrolabes. 5. Medical astrology : lunar calendars, phlebotomy instruments, astrological surgery. Moments from Thorndike Astrology reaches peak institutional power in courts and universities. Refinement of alchemical techniques; emergence of vernacular alchemy.
